one_line_takeaway: CredID reframes LLM watermarking as a multi-party trust problem: a TTP plus cooperating vendors jointly embed and vote on identity watermarks, lifting identification success to 98.5% clean / 95.1% under 50% deletion while keeping PPL overhead under 10%.
key_finding: CredID sustains a ~95% full-message success rate up to BPW 0.25 and stays above 70% at 0.32 BPW (64 bits in 200 tokens) across all three datasets, while adding negligible text-quality cost (<10% mean PPL increase). Its stated advantage over the three multi-bit baselines is holding the quality/success/capacity trade-off: Cyclic-Shift and MPAC success collapses past their max capacity and CTWL drops >20%, whereas CredID degrades gracefully. Crucially, the multi-party extraction is what delivers the headline gain: average identification success rises from 95.4% (single-party) to 98.5% (multi-party) with no attack, and from 89.7% to 95.1% under a Deletion-50% attack; per-model TPR rises (e.g., Gemma-7B 0.944->0.980 clean, 0.900->0.960 under attack) while FPR falls, and human-written text reaches TPR 1.000.
core_contribution: CredID proposes the first *multi-party* credible watermarking framework for LLM identity recognition, coupling a Trusted Third Party (TTP) with multiple LLM vendors. The paper's own three contributions are: (1) Framework — a TTP assigns each vendor a secret identity message m-hat, hashes it into a watermark seed h(m-hat) and returns only the seed to the vendor (never the user prompt or logits); at extraction the TTP distributes a seed table to one or more vendors, who report per-seed confidence, and the TTP picks the highest-confidence seed / uses multi-party voting to adjudicate text ownership. This adds public verifiability and forgery/tamper resistance that single-vendor private watermarking cannot offer. (2) Algorithm — an adapted multi-bit logit-modification watermark that adds three of its own techniques: a message 'temperature' parameter tau to sharpen the gap between the target message's logit and other messages, holistic (whole-message) encoding to raise information capacity, and a spike-entropy threshold plus retention of some high-logit tokens so low-entropy positions are bypassed to preserve text quality. (3) Tool — an open-source multi-bit watermarking toolkit with pipelines, configs, datasets and baseline algorithms. NOTE: the red/green-list logit bias, the CTWL mean-over-messages simplification, and cyclic-shift/position-encoding schemes described in Sections 2 and Appendix A are prior/cited work, not CredID's own novelty.
threat_model: Assets: the provenance/identity of LLM-generated text and the integrity of the embedded identity watermark. Adversary goals: (a) strip or corrupt the watermark so text cannot be traced to its originating LLM vendor, and (b) in the framework threat model, forge or tamper with a watermark or dispute ownership when a single vendor self-verifies (no public credibility). Adversary capabilities modeled empirically: text-editing attacks at varying corruption ratios — random token Deletion, Substitution (RoBERTa-based word replacement), Homoglyph substitution (visually similar characters), and Copy-paste / mixing watermarked text into human-written text. Privacy constraint: the framework is also designed so the TTP-vendor channel leaks only watermark seeds, protecting user prompts and vendor logit outputs. False-positive control: human-written text is flagged only if P(m|x) exceeds a threshold (10^-4 in copy-paste detection, 0.5 confidence in multi-LLM identification).
limitations: (1) Requires a genuinely trusted third party and cooperating vendors that run their own private watermarking on the shared seed — a strong deployment/coordination assumption not validated with real vendors. (2) Extraction cost scales with the message space: Alg. 2 traverses all n candidate messages x K tokens, and each vendor re-runs the token-subset selection, so multi-party voting multiplies inference cost (paper reports accuracy gains but limited efficiency analysis in the extracted pages). (3) Robustness is shown only against deletion/substitution/homoglyph/copy-paste at bounded ratios; stronger paraphrasing or adaptive/removal attacks by a knowledgeable adversary are not evaluated. (4) The general-capability impact, though small on average, is non-trivial on some benchmarks (e.g., TruthfulQA drops for Gemma-7B 38.17->30.25 and Qwen-72B 60.23->51.08 with watermark), so watermarking is not fully quality-neutral. (5) The multi-bit algorithm builds heavily on CTWL/Kirchenbauer red-green lists; novelty is incremental at the algorithm level, with the framework being the main new idea.

datasets_used: OpenGen (3,000 two-sentence blocks from WikiText-103 validation)
General-capability benchmarks: ARC (25-shot), HellaSwag (10-shot), MMLU (5-shot), GSM8K (5-shot), TruthfulQA (0-shot) via OpenLLMLeaderboard OSQ framework
quantitative_results: Success rate ~95% at BPW <= 0.1, ~90% at BPW = 0.2, and maintained 95% even at BPW 0.25; stays >70% at 0.32 BPW (64 bits / 200 tokens) on all three datasets
Watermark adds <10% mean perplexity increase (oracle: LLaMA3-70B)

Table 2 (LLaMA2-7B, C4, 500 seqs): Beam search no-attack success 0.992 / PPL 2.79; Sampling no-attack 0.990 / PPL 4.93; under 20% attacks beam-search success = Deletion 0.986, Substitution 0.946, Homoglyph 0.934, Copy-paste 0.962

Table 3 multi-party vs single-party identification: average success 95.4% -> 98.5% (clean), 89.7% -> 95.1% (Deletion-50%); Gemma-7B TPR 0.944/0.900 -> 0.980/0.960; LLaMA2-7B 0.964/0.884 -> 0.988/0.948; Falcon-7B 0.956/0.908 -> 0.988/0.944; Human-written 0.988 -> 1.000, with FPR reduced (e.g., Gemma FPR 0.005/0.024 -> 0.003/0.009)

Table 1 general-capability impact (No WM -> WM) largest drops: Gemma-7B TruthfulQA 38.17 -> 30.25, Qwen-72B TruthfulQA 60.23 -> 51.08, Falcon-7B MMLU 27.79 -> 23.12; HellaSwag essentially unchanged (e.g., Falcon-7B 85.25 -> 85.25)
Baseline degradation cited by paper: Cyclic-Shift and MPAC success drop sharply past max capacity; CTWL drops >20% with increasing capacity

Multi-LLM identification dataset: 1,000 texts x 200 tokens (250 human-written + 3 LLMs x 250 each), 20-bit identity messages, confidence threshold 0.5; copy-paste detection tested 10,000 non-watermarked texts with P(m|x) < 10^-4 threshold
baselines_compared: MPAC (Yoo et al., 'Advancing Beyond Identification: Multi-bit Watermark', ref [43])
CTWL (Wang et al., 'Towards Codable Watermarking', ICLR 2024, ref [39])
